title: Remove greater right
description: Remove nodes having greater value on right

examples.explanation: Start with head = 12. Check node 12: traverse right nodes [15,10,11,5,6,2,3]. Found 15 > 12, so remove 12. New head = 15. Check node 15: traverse right [10,11,5,6,2,3]. No value > 15, keep 15. Check node 10: traverse right [11,5,6,2,3]. Found 11 > 10, remove 10. List: 15→11→5→6→2→3. Check node 11: traverse right [5,6,2,3]. No value > 11, keep 11. Check node 5: traverse right [6,2,3]. Found 6 > 5, remove 5. List: 15→11→6→2→3. Check node 6: traverse right [2,3]. No value > 6, keep 6. Check node 2: traverse right [3]. Found 3 > 2, remove 2. List: 15→11→6→3. Check node 3: no right nodes, keep 3. Final: 15→11→6→3.

Start with head = 5. Check node 5: traverse right [4,3,2,1]. No value > 5, keep 5. Check node 4: traverse right [3,2,1]. No value > 4, keep 4. Check node 3: traverse right [2,1]. No value > 3, keep 3. Check node 2: traverse right [1]. No value > 2, keep 2. Check node 1: no right nodes, keep 1. Final list unchanged: 5→4→3→2→1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each node, traverse all nodes to its right to check if any has a greater value; if found, remove the current node. This is straightforward but inefficient for large lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Nodes with Greater Values on Right Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution that removes all nodes where any node to the right has a greater value.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach for removing nodes with greater values on the right. The intuition is straightforward: for each node in the linked list, we need to check all subsequent nodes to see if any have a greater value. If such a node exists, the current node must be removed because it violates the condition of having no greater values to its right. This approach works by systematically examining every possible pair (current node, right node) to make removal decisions. The algorithm starts from the head and moves through the list. For each current node, it initiates a second pointer that traverses all nodes to the right, comparing values. If a greater value is found, the current node is removed by adjusting pointers from the previous node (or updating head if it's the first node). After checking all nodes, the modified list contains only nodes where no right node has a greater value. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n nodes, we potentially traverse up to n nodes to the right, resulting in n*(n-1)/2 comparisons in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which handles the removal logic for a single node. Common mistakes include not properly handling head removal, losing reference to the list when removing nodes, or incorrect pointer updates that break the list structure. The brute force nature makes it inefficient for large lists but provides a clear, understandable solution.

examples.trace: Start with head = 12. Check node 12: traverse right nodes [15,10,11,5,6,2,3]. Found 15 > 12, so remove 12. New head = 15. Check node 15: traverse right [10,11,5,6,2,3]. No value > 15, keep 15. Check node 10: traverse right [11,5,6,2,3]. Found 11 > 10, remove 10. List: 15→11→5→6→2→3. Check node 11: traverse right [5,6,2,3]. No value > 11, keep 11. Check node 5: traverse right [6,2,3]. Found 6 > 5, remove 5. List: 15→11→6→2→3. Check node 6: traverse right [2,3]. No value > 6, keep 6. Check node 2: traverse right [3]. Found 3 > 2, remove 2. List: 15→11→6→3. Check node 3: no right nodes, keep 3. Final: 15→11→6→3.

Start with head = 5. Check node 5: traverse right [4,3,2,1]. No value > 5, keep 5. Check node 4: traverse right [3,2,1]. No value > 4, keep 4. Check node 3: traverse right [2,1]. No value > 3, keep 3. Check node 2: traverse right [1]. No value > 2, keep 2. Check node 1: no right nodes, keep 1. Final list unchanged: 5→4→3→2→1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Remove a Single Node if Greater Value Exists on Right
ladders.desc: Function that determines if a given node should be removed based on values to its right.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core component of the brute force solution. For a given node in a linked list, it checks all nodes to its right to determine if any have a greater value. If such a node is found, the current node must be removed. The intuition is that we need to examine the 'future' portion of the list relative to the current node to make a removal decision. The algorithm takes a node pointer 'current' and traverses from current.next to the end of the list using a separate pointer 'runner'. At each step, it compares runner.value with current.value. If runner.value > current.value at any point, it returns True indicating removal is needed. If the traversal completes without finding a greater value, it returns False. This subroutine is called for each node in the main algorithm (L0). Time complexity for this subroutine is O(k) where k is the number of nodes to the right of current node. In worst case, when current is the first node, k = n-1. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include not starting runner from current.next (which would compare the node with itself), or not handling the case when current is the last node (where there are no right nodes to check). This connects to L0 as the repeated application of this check for all nodes builds the complete solution.

examples.trace: Current node value = 10. Start runner at node 5 (value 5). 5 > 10? No. Move runner to node 12 (value 12). 12 > 10? Yes. Found greater value, return True. Node 10 should be removed.
Current node value = 12. Start runner at node 7 (value 7). 7 > 12? No. Move runner to node 9 (value 9). 9 > 12? No. End of list reached. No greater value found, return False. Node 12 should be kept.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traverse Right Portion to Find Maximum or Any Greater Value
ladders.desc: The technique of scanning nodes to the right to find if any value exceeds a threshold.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves traversing a portion of a linked list starting from a given node to check if any node's value exceeds a reference value. The intuition is that we need to examine a contiguous segment of the list to make comparisons. The algorithm maintains a reference value (typically the value of a node we're checking) and a pointer that moves through subsequent nodes. At each step, it compares the current node's value with the reference value. If a greater value is found, the search can terminate early (in our case, returning True). If the traversal completes without finding a greater value, we return False. This is essentially a linear search through a sublist. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the sublist being traversed. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the implementation of the check for a single node. The key insight is that we don't need to find the maximum value on the right—we just need to know if any value exceeds our reference. This allows early termination when the first greater value is found. Common mistakes include continuing the search after finding a greater value (inefficient) or incorrectly handling empty sublists (when the reference node is the last node).

examples.input: Reference value = 8, traverse from node 5 in: 5 → 10 → 3 → 7
examples.trace: Start at node 5 (value 5). Compare 5 with 8: 5 > 8? No. Move to node 10 (value 10). Compare 10 with 8: 10 > 8? Yes. Found greater value, return True immediately.

examples.input: Reference value = 12, traverse from node 7 in: 7 → 9 → 10 → 11
examples.trace: Start at node 7 (value 7). Compare 7 with 12: 7 > 12? No. Move to node 9 (value 9). Compare 9 with 12: 9 > 12? No. Move to node 10 (value 10). Compare 10 with 12: 10 > 12? No. Move to node 11 (value 11). Compare 11 with 12: 11 > 12? No. End of list reached. No value > 12 found, return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Linked List with Multiple Pointers
ladders.desc: Using two or more pointers to navigate and examine different parts of a linked list simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using multiple pointers to traverse a linked list, which is essential for comparing nodes at different positions. The intuition is that we need to maintain references to different nodes to perform comparisons between them. Typically, we use a 'current' pointer that moves through the list sequentially, and a 'runner' pointer that starts from current's position and moves ahead to examine subsequent nodes. The algorithm initializes current at the head (or a given starting point). For each position of current, we reset runner to current.next and move runner through the remaining nodes. This allows us to compare the value at current with values at runner positions. The key is maintaining both pointers and updating them correctly—current advances one node at a time, while runner traverses from current.next to the end for each current position. Time complexity depends on how we use the pointers; in our case, it leads to O(n²) when nested. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the mechanism to implement the right traversal. Common mistakes include not resetting runner for each current position, or having runner and current point to the same node initially. Proper pointer management is crucial to avoid infinite loops or missing nodes.

examples.input: List: 3 → 5 → 2 → 4, use current and runner pointers
examples.trace: Initialize: current at node 3, runner at node 5. Compare 3 and 5. Move runner to node 2, compare 3 and 2. Move runner to node 4, compare 3 and 4. Move current to node 5, reset runner to node 2. Compare 5 and 2. Move runner to node 4, compare 5 and 4. Move current to node 2, reset runner to node 4. Compare 2 and 4. Move current to node 4, runner becomes null. End.

examples.input: List: 1 → 2, use current and runner pointers
examples.trace: Initialize: current at node 1, runner at node 2. Compare 1 and 2. Move current to node 2, reset runner to null (since node 2 has no next). End.

testCases.input: List: 5 → 5 → 5, current at first 5, runner positions

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Remove a Node from Singly Linked List
ladders.desc: Delete a specific node from a singly linked list by adjusting pointers.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves removing a node from a singly linked list. In a singly linked list, each node has a value and a pointer to the next node. To remove a node, we need to adjust the pointer of the previous node to skip the node being removed and point directly to the next node. The intuition is that we bypass the node by linking its predecessor to its successor. The algorithm requires having access to the node to remove (current) and its previous node (prev). If prev exists (i.e., current is not the head), we set prev.next = current.next. If current is the head (no previous node), we update the head pointer to current.next. After adjusting pointers, the node is effectively removed from the list (though memory deallocation depends on language). Time complexity is O(1) for the pointer adjustment, but finding the previous node might require traversal. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as we need to remove nodes identified during traversal. Common mistakes include not handling head removal specially, losing reference to the list when removing the head, or not updating pointers correctly which can cause memory leaks or broken lists. It's crucial to maintain a reference to the node before the one being removed.

examples.trace: Identify node to remove: node with value 10. Previous node is 5. Set prev.next (5.next) to current.next (10.next which is 15). Now 5 points to 15. Node 10 is removed from the list.
Identify node to remove: head node with value 8. There is no previous node. Update head pointer to point to current.next (8.next which is 12). Now head points to 12. Node 8 is removed from the list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Traverse Singly Linked List
ladders.desc: Basic operation of visiting each node in a linked list sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept of traversing a singly linked list from beginning to end. A singly linked list consists of nodes where each node contains a value and a pointer (often called 'next') to the following node. The list starts with a head pointer that points to the first node. The last node's next pointer is null, indicating the end. Traversal involves starting at the head and following the next pointers until reaching null. The intuition is that we move through the list one node at a time, accessing each node's value or performing operations on it. The algorithm initializes a current pointer to head. While current is not null, we process current node (e.g., print its value), then set current = current.next to move to the next node. This continues until current becomes null. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a single pointer. This connects to L4 as traversal is needed to find nodes to remove. Common mistakes include not checking for null pointers (which can cause errors), or modifying the list during traversal without care. Beginners should understand that we can only move forward in a singly linked list, not backward.

examples.trace: Start: current = head (node 3). Process node 3. Move current to node 3.next (node 7). Process node 7. Move current to node 7.next (node 2). Process node 2. Move current to node 2.next (null). End traversal.
Start: current = head (node 5). Process node 5. Move current to node 5.next (null). End traversal.

approaches.name: Reverse Traversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Reverse the linked list, then traverse from left to right while keeping track of the maximum value seen so far; remove nodes with values less than the maximum. Finally, reverse the list back to original order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Nodes Having Greater Value on Right
ladders.desc: Complete solution using reverse traversal approach to remove all nodes where a greater value exists to their right.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that by reversing the linked list, we transform the problem from 'remove nodes with greater values on the right' to 'remove nodes with smaller values on the left' relative to the traversal direction. This is crucial because in a singly linked list, we can only traverse forward, making it difficult to compare each node with all nodes to its right. After reversal, we traverse from what was originally the tail (now head) to what was originally the head (now tail). During this traversal, we maintain a running maximum of values seen so far. Any node with a value less than this maximum must have had a greater value to its right in the original list, so we remove it. Finally, we reverse the list back to restore the original order with the appropriate nodes removed.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Reverse the entire linked list
2. Initialize max_val = -infinity and current pointer at new head
3. Traverse the reversed list:
   - If current.value < max_val: remove current node
   - Else: update max_val = max(max_val, current.value)
   - Move to next node
4. Reverse the list again to restore original order
5. Return the head of the modified list

Time complexity: O(n) for three passes (reverse, traverse, reverse)
Space complexity: O(1) using only constant extra space

Connection to L1: This full solution combines the three key sub-routines from L1: list reversal, traversal with maximum tracking, and node removal.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle edge cases like empty list or single node
2. Not properly updating pointers when removing nodes during traversal
3. Incorrectly reversing the list (especially the second reversal)
4. Not resetting max_val properly for each test case

Real code pattern:
def remove_greater_right(head):
    if not head or not head.next:
        return head
    
    # First reversal
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = next_node
    reversed_head = prev
    
    # Traversal with removal
    max_val = float('-inf')
    curr = reversed_head
    dummy = ListNode(0, reversed_head)
    prev = dummy
    
    while curr:
        if curr.val < max_val:
            prev.next = curr.next
            curr = curr.next
        else:
            max_val = max(max_val, curr.val)
            prev = curr
            curr = curr.next
    
    # Second reversal
    new_head = dummy.next
    prev = None
    curr = new_head
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = next_node
    
    return prev

examples.trace:
Step 1: Reverse list: 3 → 2 → 6 → 5 → 11 → 10 → 15 → 12
Step 2: Traverse reversed list with max tracking:
- Start: max_val = -∞, curr = 3, keep (3 > -∞), max_val = 3
- curr = 2, remove (2 < 3)
- curr = 6, keep (6 > 3), max_val = 6
- curr = 5, remove (5 < 6)
- curr = 11, keep (11 > 6), max_val = 11
- curr = 10, remove (10 < 11)
- curr = 15, keep (15 > 11), max_val = 15
- curr = 12, remove (12 < 15)
Result after traversal: 3 → 6 → 11 → 15
Step 3: Reverse back: 15 → 11 → 6 → 3

Step 1: Reverse list: 8 → 3 → 13 → 2 → 5
Step 2: Traverse reversed list with max tracking:
- Start: max_val = -∞, curr = 8, keep (8 > -∞), max_val = 8
- curr = 3, remove (3 < 8)
- curr = 13, keep (13 > 8), max_val = 13
- curr = 2, remove (2 < 13)
- curr = 5, remove (5 < 13)
Result after traversal: 8 → 13
Step 3: Reverse back: 13 → 8

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse Reversed List with Maximum Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list while maintaining maximum value seen and removing nodes with values less than current maximum.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core algorithmic step where we implement the main logic of identifying which nodes to remove. After reversing the list, we traverse it from what was originally the tail (now head) to what was originally the head (now tail). The key insight is that during this traversal, any node with a value less than the maximum value seen so far must have had a greater value to its right in the original list. This works because in the reversed list, we're essentially moving from right to left in the original list, so we're comparing each node with all nodes that were originally to its right.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize max_val to negative infinity (or minimum possible value)
2. Create a dummy node pointing to head for easier edge case handling
3. Initialize prev = dummy and curr = head
4. While curr is not null:
   a. If curr.value < max_val:
      - Remove curr by setting prev.next = curr.next
      - Move curr to curr.next
   b. Else:
      - Update max_val = max(max_val, curr.value)
      - Move prev to curr
      - Move curr to curr.next
5. Return dummy.next as the new head

Time complexity: O(n) for single traversal
Space complexity: O(1) using only constant extra space

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is the central processing step in the full solution, sandwiched between two list reversals.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to initialize max_val properly
2. Not handling the removal correctly (losing reference to next node)
3. Incorrectly updating prev pointer only when not removing
4. Not using a dummy node makes head removal cases tricky

Real code pattern:
def traverse_with_max_removal(head):
    if not head:
        return None
    
    max_val = float('-inf')
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    prev = dummy
    curr = head
    
    while curr:
        if curr.val < max_val:
            prev.next = curr.next
            curr = curr.next
        else:
            max_val = max(max_val, curr.val)
            prev = curr
            curr = curr.next
    
    return dummy.next

examples.trace:
Start: max_val = -∞, dummy → 3 → 2 → 6 → 5 → 11 → 10 → 15 → 12
Step 1: curr = 3, 3 > -∞, keep, max_val = 3, prev = 3
Step 2: curr = 2, 2 < 3, remove, prev.next = 6, list: 3 → 6 → 5 → 11 → 10 → 15 → 12
Step 3: curr = 6, 6 > 3, keep, max_val = 6, prev = 6
Step 4: curr = 5, 5 < 6, remove, prev.next = 11, list: 3 → 6 → 11 → 10 → 15 → 12
Step 5: curr = 11, 11 > 6, keep, max_val = 11, prev = 11
Step 6: curr = 10, 10 < 11, remove, prev.next = 15, list: 3 → 6 → 11 → 15 → 12
Step 7: curr = 15, 15 > 11, keep, max_val = 15, prev = 15
Step 8: curr = 12, 12 < 15, remove, prev.next = None
Final: 3 → 6 → 11 → 15

Start: max_val = -∞, dummy → 8 → 3 → 13 → 2 → 5
Step 1: curr = 8, 8 > -∞, keep, max_val = 8, prev = 8
Step 2: curr = 3, 3 < 8, remove, prev.next = 13, list: 8 → 13 → 2 → 5
Step 3: curr = 13, 13 > 8, keep, max_val = 13, prev = 13
Step 4: curr = 2, 2 < 13, remove, prev.next = 5, list: 8 → 13 → 5
Step 5: curr = 5, 5 < 13, remove, prev.next = None
Final: 8 → 13

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Maximum During Traversal
ladders.desc: Track maximum value seen so far while traversing a linked list and identify nodes with values less than this maximum.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental insight that makes the reverse traversal approach work. The core idea is that by traversing the reversed list from right to left (relative to the original list), we can use a simple maximum-tracking mechanism to identify which nodes to remove. In the original problem, we need to remove nodes that have any greater value to their right. After reversal, this becomes: remove nodes that have a value less than the maximum value seen so far during traversal. This works because when we traverse the reversed list, we're essentially moving backward through the original list. The maximum value we've seen represents the greatest value among all nodes that were originally to the right of the current node.

Step-by-step logic:
1. Initialize max_so_far to smallest possible value
2. For each node during traversal:
   a. Compare node.value with max_so_far
   b. If node.value < max_so_far: mark for removal
   c. Else: update max_so_far = node.value
3. Continue until end of list

Time complexity: O(n) for single pass
Space complexity: O(1) for storing max_so_far

Connection to L1: This logic is implemented within the traversal sub-routine to decide which nodes to keep or remove.

Common mistakes:
1. Initializing max_so_far incorrectly (should be very small)
2. Comparing with wrong inequality (should be < not <=)
3. Not updating max_so_far when keeping a node
4. Confusing the direction of traversal in reversed vs original list

Real code pattern:
def track_maximum(head):
    max_val = float('-inf')
    result = []
    curr = head
    
    while curr:
        if curr.val >= max_val:
            result.append(curr.val)
            max_val = curr.val
        curr = curr.next
    
    return result

examples.trace:
Start: max_val = -∞
Step 1: val = 3, 3 >= -∞, keep, max_val = 3
Step 2: val = 2, 2 < 3, skip
Step 3: val = 6, 6 >= 3, keep, max_val = 6
Step 4: val = 5, 5 < 6, skip
Step 5: val = 11, 11 >= 6, keep, max_val = 11
Step 6: val = 10, 10 < 11, skip
Step 7: val = 15, 15 >= 11, keep, max_val = 15
Step 8: val = 12, 12 < 15, skip
Final kept values: [3, 6, 11, 15]

Start: max_val = -∞
Step 1: val = 8, 8 >= -∞, keep, max_val = 8
Step 2: val = 3, 3 < 8, skip
Step 3: val = 13, 13 >= 8, keep, max_val = 13
Step 4: val = 2, 2 < 13, skip
Step 5: val = 5, 5 < 13, skip
Final kept values: [8, 13]

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked List Reversal
ladders.desc: Reverse a singly linked list in-place using iterative method.
ladders.explanation:
Linked list reversal is a fundamental operation needed for this approach. We need to reverse the list twice: once before the main processing, and once after. The intuition behind list reversal is to change the direction of all next pointers. We maintain three pointers: previous (initially null), current (starts at head), and next (temporary storage). At each step, we store current.next in next, point current.next to previous, move previous to current, and move current to next. This effectively reverses the links one node at a time.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize prev = null, curr = head
2. While curr is not null:
   a. Store next node: next = curr.next
   b. Reverse link: curr.next = prev
   c. Move pointers: prev = curr, curr = next
3. Return prev as new head

Time complexity: O(n) for single pass
Space complexity: O(1) using only constant extra space

Connection to L2: List reversal enables the maximum tracking logic by allowing us to traverse from right to left in the original list.

Common mistakes:
1. Losing reference to next node before updating current.next
2. Not returning the correct new head (should be prev, not curr)
3. Forgetting to handle empty list case
4. Incorrect pointer updates order

Real code pattern:
def reverse_list(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = next_node
    
    return prev

examples.trace:
Start: prev = null, curr = 1
Step 1: next = 2, 1.next = null, prev = 1, curr = 2
Step 2: next = 3, 2.next = 1, prev = 2, curr = 3
Step 3: next = 4, 3.next = 2, prev = 3, curr = 4
Step 4: next = 5, 4.next = 3, prev = 4, curr = 5
Step 5: next = null, 5.next = 4, prev = 5, curr = null
Final: 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1

Start: prev = null, curr = 10
Step 1: next = 20, 10.next = null, prev = 10, curr = 20
Step 2: next = 30, 20.next = 10, prev = 20, curr = 30
Step 3: next = null, 30.next = 20, prev = 30, curr = null
Final: 30 → 20 → 10

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Remove Node from Linked List
ladders.desc: Delete a specific node from a singly linked list given reference to the node and its previous node.
ladders.explanation:
Removing a node from a singly linked list is a fundamental operation needed for the main algorithm. Since we only have forward pointers, we need access to the previous node to properly remove the current node. The intuition is simple: to remove node B in sequence A → B → C, we need to change A.next to point to C instead of B. This effectively removes B from the chain. If we don't have direct access to the previous node, we need to traverse from the head to find it, which would be O(n). However, in our algorithm, we maintain a 'prev' pointer during traversal, giving us O(1) removal.

Step-by-step operation:
1. Given prev node and curr node to remove
2. Update prev.next = curr.next
3. Optionally free/delete curr node memory
4. Move curr to curr.next for continued traversal

Time complexity: O(1) when prev is known
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to L3: Node removal is performed during the traversal of the reversed list after maximum comparison.

Common mistakes:
1. Trying to remove head node without special handling
2. Losing reference to next node before updating links
3. Not updating traversal pointers correctly after removal
4. Memory leaks if not properly deallocating (in languages with manual memory management)

Real code pattern:
def remove_node(prev, curr):
    if not prev or not curr:
        return
    
    prev.next = curr.next
    # In languages with manual memory management: delete/free curr
    
    # Continue traversal from next node
    return curr.next

examples.input: List: A → B → C → D, Remove node B
examples.trace:
Initial: A.next = B, B.next = C, C.next = D
Step 1: Identify prev = A, curr = B
Step 2: Update A.next = B.next (which is C)
Step 3: Now A.next points directly to C
Result: A → C → D
Note: Node B is removed from the chain

Initial: 1.next = 2, 2.next = 3, 3.next = 4
Step 1: Identify prev = 2, curr = 3
Step 2: Update 2.next = 3.next (which is 4)
Step 3: Now 2.next points directly to 4
Result: 1 → 2 → 4
Note: Node 3 is removed from the chain

testCases.input: List: a → b → c → d → e, Remove: c (prev=b)
List: x → y → z, Remove: y (prev=x)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Traversal
ladders.desc: Basic traversal of a singly linked list from head to tail.
ladders.explanation:
Linked list traversal is the most fundamental operation for working with linked lists. A singly linked list consists of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node. The list starts with a head pointer. Traversal means visiting each node exactly once, typically to process the data or modify the structure. The intuition is simple: start at the head, follow the next pointers until reaching null (end of list). This is analogous to following a chain of links. During traversal, we can perform operations like reading values, updating values, or keeping track of additional information (like maximum value in our main problem).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize current pointer = head
2. While current is not null:
   a. Process current node (read value, update, etc.)
   b. Move to next node: current = current.next
3. End when current becomes null

Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes
Space complexity: O(1) for iterative traversal

Connection to L4: Traversal is how we move through the list to find nodes to remove and to perform the maximum tracking.

Common mistakes:
1. Infinite loop by not updating current pointer
2. Accessing current.data when current is null
3. Starting traversal from wrong node
4. Not handling empty list (head = null)

Real code pattern:
def traverse_list(head):
    curr = head
    while curr:
        # Process curr.val here
        print(curr.val)
        curr = curr.next

examples.trace:
Step 1: curr = 10, process 10, curr = 20
Step 2: curr = 20, process 20, curr = 30
Step 3: curr = 30, process 30, curr = 40
Step 4: curr = 40, process 40, curr = null
Step 5: End (curr is null)
Total visited: 10, 20, 30, 40

Step 1: curr = 5, process 5, curr = 15
Step 2: curr = 15, process 15, curr = 25
Step 3: curr = 25, process 25, curr = null
Step 4: End (curr is null)
Total visited: 5, 15, 25

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the list from left to right, using a stack to store nodes; pop from the stack when a node with greater value is encountered to remove smaller nodes. This maintains the relative order while filtering nodes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Nodes Having Greater Value on Right Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to remove all nodes that have a greater value node to their right in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires removing nodes from a singly linked list where there exists any node to the right with a greater value. The stack-based approach solves this efficiently in O(n) time and O(n) space by processing nodes from left to right while maintaining a decreasing order in the stack. Intuition: We want to keep nodes in decreasing order from left to right, ensuring no node has a greater value to its right. By traversing the list and using a stack, we can compare each new node with the top of the stack (which represents the most recent kept node). If the new node is greater, we pop from the stack (removing smaller nodes that would have this greater node to their right). This process naturally filters out nodes with greater values on the right while preserving relative order. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty stack. 2. Traverse the linked list from head to tail. 3. For each node: a. While stack is not empty and current node's value > top of stack's value, pop from stack. b. Push current node onto stack. 4. After traversal, rebuild the linked list from the stack (in reverse order since stack is LIFO) to maintain original left-to-right order. 5. Return the new head. Time complexity: O(n) as each node is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity: O(n) for the stack in worst case (e.g., decreasing list). Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of stack manipulation to filter nodes. Common mistakes: Not handling the stack rebuild correctly (must reverse order), forgetting to update node pointers when popping, or missing edge cases like empty list. Real code pattern: def remove_greater_right(head): stack = []; curr = head; while curr: while stack and curr.val > stack[-1].val: stack.pop(); stack.append(curr); curr = curr.next; new_head = None; while stack: node = stack.pop(); node.next = new_head; new_head = node; return new_head.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = []. Traverse list. Node 5: stack empty, push 5 -> stack = [5]. Node 2: 2 < top 5, push 2 -> stack = [5,2]. Node 13: 13 > top 2, pop 2 -> stack = [5]; 13 > top 5, pop 5 -> stack = []; push 13 -> stack = [13]. Node 3: 3 < top 13, push 3 -> stack = [13,3]. Node 8: 8 > top 3, pop 3 -> stack = [13]; 8 < top 13, push 8 -> stack = [13,8]. End traversal. Rebuild list from stack: pop 8 -> new_head = 8; pop 13 -> 13.next = 8, new_head = 13. Result: 13 -> 8.

Step 1: stack = []. Node 1: push 1 -> stack = [1]. Node 2: 2 > top 1, pop 1 -> stack = []; push 2 -> stack = [2]. Node 3: 3 > top 2, pop 2 -> stack = []; push 3 -> stack = [3]. Node 4: 4 > top 3, pop 3 -> stack = []; push 4 -> stack = [4]. Rebuild: pop 4 -> new_head = 4. Result: 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Manipulation for Node Filtering
ladders.desc: Implement the core stack operations to filter nodes based on value comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the stack manipulation logic that removes nodes with greater values on the right. Intuition: By maintaining a stack of nodes in decreasing order, we ensure that when we encounter a node with a greater value, we remove all smaller nodes from the stack (since they have this greater node to their right). This is the heart of the O(n) algorithm. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty stack. 2. For each node in the list (traverse left to right): a. While stack is not empty and current node value > top of stack value, pop from stack. b. Push current node onto stack. 3. The stack now contains nodes in decreasing order, with all nodes that had greater values on the right removed. Time complexity: O(n) as each node is pushed once and popped at most once. Space complexity: O(n) for the stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine is used directly in the full solution to filter nodes before rebuilding the list. Connection to L2: It relies on the core insight of maintaining decreasing order. Common mistakes: Not using while loop for multiple pops (only popping once), incorrect comparison (e.g., using >= instead of > which might remove equal values unnecessarily), or not handling empty stack case. Real code pattern: def filter_nodes(head): stack = []; curr = head; while curr: while stack and curr.val > stack[-1].val: stack.pop(); stack.append(curr); curr = curr.next; return stack.

examples.trace: Start stack = []. Process node 5: stack empty, push 5 -> stack = [5]. Node 2: 2 < 5, push 2 -> stack = [5,2]. Node 13: while stack and 13 > 2: pop 2 -> stack = [5]; while stack and 13 > 5: pop 5 -> stack = []; push 13 -> stack = [13]. Node 3: 3 < 13, push 3 -> stack = [13,3]. Node 8: while stack and 8 > 3: pop 3 -> stack = [13]; 8 < 13, push 8 -> stack = [13,8]. Final stack = [13,8].

Stack = []. Node 1: push 1 -> [1]. Node 2: while stack and 2 > 1: pop 1 -> []; push 2 -> [2]. Node 3: while stack and 3 > 2: pop 2 -> []; push 3 -> [3]. Node 4: while stack and 4 > 3: pop 3 -> []; push 4 -> [4]. Final stack = [4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Decreasing Order with Stack
ladders.desc: Understand the technique of using a stack to keep elements in decreasing order while traversing.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core algorithmic insight: using a stack to maintain a decreasing sequence of values as we process a list from left to right. Intuition: For the problem of removing nodes with greater values on the right, we need to ensure that in the final list, every node is greater than or equal to all nodes to its right (i.e., decreasing order). A stack is perfect for this because it allows us to compare each new element with the most recent kept elements (top of stack) and remove smaller ones efficiently. Why it works: By popping smaller elements when a larger one is encountered, we eliminate nodes that would have a greater value to their right. The stack naturally preserves the left-to-right order of kept nodes. Step-by-step: 1. Start with empty stack. 2. For each element x: a. While stack not empty and x > top of stack, pop from stack. b. Push x onto stack. 3. The stack now holds elements in decreasing order. Time complexity: O(n) as each element is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity: O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the stack manipulation subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on the concept of stack LIFO behavior. Common mistakes: Misunderstanding the order (should be decreasing, not increasing), not realizing that popping can happen multiple times per element, or confusing with monotonic stack problems. Real code pattern: def decreasing_stack(arr): stack = []; for num in arr: while stack and num > stack[-1]: stack.pop(); stack.append(num); return stack.

examples.trace: Process 5: stack=[], push 5 -> [5]. Process 2: 2 < 5, push 2 -> [5,2]. Process 13: 13 > 2, pop 2 -> [5]; 13 > 5, pop 5 -> []; push 13 -> [13]. Process 3: 3 < 13, push 3 -> [13,3]. Process 8: 8 > 3, pop 3 -> [13]; 8 < 13, push 8 -> [13,8]. Final stack = [13,8].
Process 1: push 1 -> [1]. Process 2: 2 > 1, pop 1 -> []; push 2 -> [2]. Process 3: 3 > 2, pop 2 -> []; push 3 -> [3]. Process 4: 4 > 3, pop 3 -> []; push 4 -> [4]. Final stack = [4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations with Comparisons
ladders.desc: Practice basic stack operations (push, pop, peek) combined with value comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the fundamental skill of using a stack data structure while comparing values between the current element and the top of the stack. Intuition: Stacks follow LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) order, making them ideal for problems where we need to process elements sequentially and look back at recent elements. By comparing the current value with the top of the stack, we can make decisions about removing elements (popping) or keeping them (pushing). This is a common pattern in algorithms like monotonic stacks. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty stack. 2. Iterate through a list of numbers. 3. For each number: a. Check if stack is not empty and compare current number with stack's top element. b. If condition met (e.g., current > top), pop from stack. c. Push current number onto stack. 4. The stack evolves based on comparisons. Time complexity: O(n) for n elements. Space complexity: O(n) for stack storage. Connection to L2: This building block is used to maintain decreasing order. Connection to L4: It relies on basic stack push/pop operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check if stack is empty before peeking (causing errors), incorrect comparison logic, or not using while loop for multiple pops. Real code pattern: stack = []; for num in nums: while stack and num > stack[-1]: stack.pop(); stack.append(num).

examples.input: Process [3, 1, 4, 2] with condition: pop if current > top
examples.trace: Start stack = []. Num 3: stack empty, push 3 -> [3]. Num 1: 1 < top 3, push 1 -> [3,1]. Num 4: 4 > top 1, pop 1 -> [3]; 4 > top 3, pop 3 -> []; push 4 -> [4]. Num 2: 2 < top 4, push 2 -> [4,2]. Final stack = [4,2].

examples.input: Process [5, 4, 3, 2, 1] with condition: pop if current > top
examples.trace: Stack = []. Num 5: push 5 -> [5]. Num 4: 4 < 5, push 4 -> [5,4]. Num 3: 3 < 4, push 3 -> [5,4,3]. Num 2: 2 < 3, push 2 -> [5,4,3,2]. Num 1: 1 < 2, push 1 -> [5,4,3,2,1]. No pops occur as condition never met. Final stack = [5,4,3,2,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with List
ladders.desc: Implement fundamental stack operations using a list in Python.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of a stack data structure using a list, which is essential for the overall algorithm. Intuition: A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) principle. In Python, a list can simulate a stack efficiently: append() acts as push (add to top), pop() removes from top, and accessing the last element with [-1] acts as peek. Understanding these operations is crucial for implementing more complex algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty list to represent the stack. 2. Push operation: Use list.append(item) to add an element to the top. 3. Pop operation: Use list.pop() to remove and return the top element. 4. Peek operation: Use list[-1] to view the top element without removing it. 5. Check if empty: Use len(list) == 0 or not list. Time complexity: O(1) for push, pop, and peek. Space complexity: O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to L3: These operations are used in stack comparisons. Connection to L5: It builds on basic list manipulation. Common mistakes: Using pop(0) instead of pop() (which removes from front, not top), forgetting to check if stack is empty before pop or peek, or misusing index -1. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(5); top = stack[-1]; popped = stack.pop(); is_empty = len(stack) == 0.

examples.input: Push 1, 2, 3 then pop twice, then push 4
examples.trace: Start stack = []. Push 1: append 1 -> stack = [1]. Push 2: append 2 -> stack = [1,2]. Push 3: append 3 -> stack = [1,2,3]. Pop: remove top 3 -> stack = [1,2], popped value = 3. Pop: remove top 2 -> stack = [1], popped value = 2. Push 4: append 4 -> stack = [1,4]. Final stack = [1,4].
Stack = []. Push 5: append 5 -> [5]. Peek: top = 5 (stack[-1]). Pop: remove 5 -> [], popped value = 5. Push 6: append 6 -> [6]. Peek: top = 6. Final stack = [6].

testCases.input: Push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop, push 4
Push 2, push 3, push 4, pop, pop, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Introduction to Lists and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Learn basic list operations in Python: creation, appending, and accessing elements.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the foundational concept of lists in Python, which are used to implement stacks in the algorithm. Intuition: A list is an ordered collection of items that can be modified. It is a fundamental data structure in programming, and understanding how to create, add to, and access elements is essential for building more complex algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Create an empty list: my_list = []. 2. Append an element to the end: my_list.append(value). 3. Access elements by index: my_list[index] where index starts at 0 for the first element, and -1 for the last element. 4. Check length: len(my_list). 5. Iterate through list: for item in my_list. Time complexity: O(1) for append and access by index. Space complexity: O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to L4: Lists are used to implement stack operations. Connection to broader context: This is a prerequisite for any data structure manipulation. Common mistakes: Confusing index positions (off-by-one errors), using wrong methods for adding elements (e.g., insert instead of append), or not initializing lists properly. Real code pattern: lst = []; lst.append(10); first = lst[0]; last = lst[-1]; size = len(lst); for x in lst: print(x).

examples.input: Create list, append 5 and 3, access first and last elements
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty list: lst = []. Step 2: Append 5: lst.append(5) -> lst = [5]. Step 3: Append 3: lst.append(3) -> lst = [5,3]. Step 4: Access first element: lst[0] = 5. Step 5: Access last element: lst[-1] = 3 or lst[1] = 3. Final list = [5,3].

examples.input: Create list with 1,2,3, check length, iterate to print
examples.trace: Step 1: Create list: lst = [1,2,3]. Step 2: Check length: len(lst) = 3. Step 3: Iterate: for x in lst: print(x) outputs 1, then 2, then 3. Final list unchanged: [1,2,3].

testCases.input: Create list with 0, append 1, access index 0
List [3], access last, append 4, access last
